speaker: interviewer
text: Can you hear the TV?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [LAUGHING] Take it!

speaker: interviewer
text: Now just read it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just [? repeat ?] it. [? Just repeat ?] it already.

speaker: interviewer
text: July 11, 1995-- Eugene Fried is the survivor. Lilian Gordon is the interviewer. We're in Cranbury, New Jersey, in the United States. And we'll be speaking in English. I'm Lilian Gordon. We're in Cranbury, New Jersey. And I'll be interviewing Eugene Fried, who is a survivor of the Holocaust. And we're in the United States. Fried.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eugene-- is was-- name was born was [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE] Fried in Czechoslovakia, was [? Amgen ?] Fried. That was my school name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and when were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Hebrew name was [? Yiedle. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Yiedle? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Yiedle, ?] and in the Hebrew means [? Yejuda ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah. And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: August 18, 1926 in Mukacevo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-U-K-A-C-A-- Mukacaevo-- E-V-O-- Mukacevo.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what country was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have another name, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, then it was-- in Hungarian, when the Hungarian came, it was Munkc, M-U-N-K-C-- Munkc-- just with a c. But they used to make an E [? at the end, ?] Munkc.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were born to a husband and wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my father and mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father name was Pinkus [INAUDIBLE] Fried, but Pinkus Ede-- E-D-E-- Ede. My mother's name was Leah in Jewish. And the Heb-- and the English was-- I mean, in English, it would have been [? Lanka ?] Fried. Her maiden name was Tindel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you spell that maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name, T-I-N-D-E-L-- Tindel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately when were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: They must have been born in the 1800s, because-- last, the latest-- 1900, beginning of 1900. Because they were in early 40s when they took us away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't know their exact dates?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exact names. I know there were in early 40s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody live with you? Did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- yeah, we have, I have, we have six children. My two br-- there were four brothers and two sisters, which two little brothers did not come, not survive. And my parents did not survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the names of, of your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brothers was-- one, the oldest one was Nathan. He did not survive. And the youngest one was Heshey.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Nathan was, was born when?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born 19-- he was 10 years old when they took him away. He was born in 1941. And was '30-- and the other one was born in 1939-- the first one, yeah, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your oldest brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: William. His name was [INAUDIBLE], his Jewish name. His name is William Fried.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was born in May, 1923?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you had two sisters also?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two sisters, yeah. They're, they're both alive. My older sister was born 1924. I think was October.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Margaret Fried. And the younger sister was Irene. Her Hebrew name is-- in, in Czech, it was [INAUDIBLE], same thing as over here-- Irene. And [? Hinda ?] was her Jewish name. [? Hinda? ?] [? Hinda, ?] yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was born in 1928?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1928, Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. Now what-- before the, before you were occupied, what was your life like? Were you-- What kind of religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were just working. My parents used to-- they used to sew clothing, ladies' clothing. And they used to sell it. It was for like for a flea market. They used to  sell them twice a week, Wednesday and Friday. They used to sell the clothes in Munkc. And then in 1930-- when I was about seven years old, we moved to [INAUDIBLE], like in the suburb of [INAUDIBLE]. It's right across the bridge, about a half a mile, not more. That's where we had opened up a store. We had a store.

speaker: interviewer
text: We had grocery and deli there and the clothing, too. But the clothing was only, like I said, twice a week. So we had mostly grocery, deli, and vegetables, things like that, you know? So you would sell the, the vegetables in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And groceries, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Flea market, also, or just in the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just, just in the store. But the flea market was only with the clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you live in an exclusively Jewish area then, or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: An integrated area?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was integrated, yeah. It was mostly, mostly Jewish, but was integrate-- [? Croatian, ?] Jewish, Christian, you know, was a mix. Because there were Hungarians. There were other things. There were Czechs. And there was like some German-- like [? Swabs, we ?] used to call them. It was a mixed integrate. But everybody got along peaceful. Nobody was any-- before the Hungarians started all that, there was not, no-- there was never any fights with them or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean before the Hungarians started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the Hungarians occupied us in 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: So your city was occupied by Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was 1938 or '39. That's when the, when the Allied made a pact his Hitler, they divided-- they cut up Czechoslovakia like the Sudeten. The upper Czechoslovakia, they gave them to the, to the Germans. And the Carpathian was the lower part of Czechoslovakia. They gave it the Hungarians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your life change after that pact at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much, very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- did you go to school before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school in the Czech school. And I went through 5th grade. Till, up to the 5th grade, I went Czech school. And then in '30, '39, then I start to another school, Hungarian-- brand new life, completely different life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were about 13 when that pact was signed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- 13, no. No, was 1938. I was about 12, about 12. 19-- I was born 1926. 1938, was about, was about 12, 12 or 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before that, had you had any incidents of anti-Semitism that you knew of?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we never had any problems with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then after the pact was signed, how did your life change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Hungarians took over. Then they started like-- from the beginning, was all right. But not, you know, it was-- but then they came out. The Hungarians made the [INAUDIBLE] They made special laws for the Jewish people. Now, for instance, they-- if Jewish people had a store, like with groceries, they keep when-- during the war, they gave you food and rationings, you know? They give you little cards, you know, papers for-- coupons like. But they took away from the Jews, The Jewish stores couldn't, they couldn't sell it. But first of all, they took away all the, the permits from the Jewish people from the stores. So we couldn't sell. And whoever left the store, the permit, they couldn't sell anything. But it was rationed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was in 1939 before--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939, Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before Hitler was involved at all in, in, in your country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was Hungarian-- Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was just the Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, what they-- they were like [INAUDIBLE]. They would also-- they, they sided with the Germans, the Hungarians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did your father and mother support the six of you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they still had the-- what do you call it-- the clothing little bit, you know? Then just a little bit of the groceries, just whatever we could sell without the, without the rations we sold. It was hard. But we grew up. You know, my brother started to work a little bit. And my sister started working. And I started, too. I was about 12 or 13. I started to work in a bakery, help, you know, after school. I went to work in a bakery. So I brought home a little bread. Everybody worked a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- were you able to go to the same school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, for the [INAUDIBLE], yes. But it was one school yet. But the Czech was-- everybody was mixed. But then with Hungarians was also mixed yet. But mostly, it was Jewish people was in the-- one school. And the others was [? Rutines ?], the Ru-- like Russian. It's Ukraine [INAUDIBLE]. They has their separate schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- how observant was your family? I know your town was a very--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was religious, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Large.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was kosher. It was a kosher home all the time. Yeah, like Sabbath, we did not do-- work any Sabbath. Like Friday night, everything ceased. We did not work Friday or Saturday or holidays, any.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your families followers of any Hasidic group?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no we just-- regular, you know, no, not Hasidic. But they were relig-- observant. That's all, not really fanatic, plain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to any school after school to--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Hebrew school after the-- up till the Hungarians came in. Then [INAUDIBLE] disrupted. I couldn't. You have to go all the way to Munkc, you know, to the city. And then we couldn't afford it anyway anymore, either. So I stopped going to Hebrew school when I was about 12, 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you like to do when you were a child in, in your city?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to play with the kids, like, you know, not much. And I worked, helped my mother in the store a lot of times. And I used to go with her shopping, you know, used to run with the errands, things like that. I used to-- like a young child, you know, play around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hang out with your brother at all-- your older brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to hang, hang out with brother, too. Because, you see, we had like-- in the house-- they had like steps from the house. They had steps. We used to get together at night. And a kid used to come here, play the guitar. They used to-- matter of fact, he wasn't even Jewish, very nice [INAUDIBLE] used to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: He used to come-- together all kids. Don't make-- didn't make any difference, kids together. And we used to play, he used to play the harm-- the guitar. And we used to sing together on the step. They'll sing. And it was-- we're happy, you know? Did your younger brothers follow you, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were, they were still young. They were, but, you know, but they-- we played together, you know? But they had their own friends. They were young kids. You know, we were, we didn't-- not too much luxury. But it was just living, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a lot of extended family around-- aunts and uncles and grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had a lot. We had uncles. Yeah, we had about-- extended family, about, say about-- real extended, could have been about 200.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but let's say-- that's if you mean like cousins and cousins of cousins, like this. And real close, I've got about 80. The uncles and cousins, and we had about 60 or 80. And from that, we had about-- we came-- till the concentration camp, we had like-- my uncle came back. When my uncle, the doctor, came back--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, we'll get to that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- oh. Till then, about something like that, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody else live with you? I think--

speaker: interviewer
text: My grandfather used to live with us, because when Hungarians came in, you know, they lost their business. So he came to live with us for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you could take him into your apartment? You had a large enough--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we took him into our apartment, yes. And then when the war started, my uncle, the doctor who bought the house, he came back from-- he was in the Sudetenland. Like I told you, the Germans took over. He came back. And he took over the upstairs floor. He had an office up there, a doctor. And then later on, he moved out to the city. So we went back upstairs, moved back upstairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war, what did you think you were going to be when you were grown up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was hoping to be a doctor. Because remember, like today, in school they used to say, like make essays about the-- what would you like to be when you grow up. And you know, I, I remember, like today, I made an essay. I would like to be a doctor, but never the chance. Because I quit school when I was 14. I don't go any more schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you quit school?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, it was a new language. Hungarian was a new language. I used to use the Czechoslovakian language. And we used to speak Yiddish. And then already, it was hard. We had to-- I h ad to go to work when I was-- I was, I started working when I was about 12 or younger than. So we couldn't go to any high school anyway. Because first of all, you couldn't go, because Jewish people, they couldn't go to high school anyway. So every-- the whole life was disrupted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were Jewish people not allowed to go to high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- high school, they still, they still went. No, just to eighth grade, that's all-- eighth grade, most of them. There was already the war. There was the war already. So we just-- everybody-- whoever was there, they started to work to make a living and help out to the family. So it just was not-- no chance to go to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where would you get enough money? I know you said you worked as-- in a bakery.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in a bakery. I used to get paid--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brother work, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother worked. He was a, a book binder. He was learning a trade, a book-- was a book binder. So he bring-- he brought a little bit. My sister started-- my sister started to work a little bit. So a little bit, and we had-- still had the store. So we made-- it was a living, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So how would you get the ration coupons?

speaker: interviewee
text: They give you-- every week, they give you ration coupons. So that's what-- they didn't give you no meats or things that. They'd just give you, like, milk or eggs and some-- eggs, you could buy. But the people from the towns used to come in. They used give you-- they used to bring and sell the eggs. And we used to buy it from them. But you still have mostly-- a lot of fruit was there and bread. And that's, that's how we used to live most of the time. Because it was, it was like-- it was not far from the people from the, from the farms. They used to bring and to sell it, so we used to buy it from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you, you could buy--

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to trade from the  store something for them, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and then what happened in terms of your life? What happened when Hitler came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then when-- now, the Hungarian-- after Hung-- a year, it was all right with Hungarians. After a year, they started to take the Jewish people like to, to work. First of all, they drafted the men. All the men, they drafted to the-- they used to call it [INAUDIBLE], labor camps. And the kids, from 12, they used to call it [INAUDIBLE] . It's like, over here would be-- not like Boy Scouts, but they used to train like military.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I [INAUDIBLE]. 12-Year-old, you have to be there. And first, for a while, we went together with the Christian kids. After that, they separated Christian kids. And the Jewish kids, they took to the fields to work. And over there, they had, like, you know, them the people in charge,

speaker: interviewer
text: The people that were in charge, they were like, taking-- what do you call it-- were in charge we should work digging ditches and things like that. And they were-- like animals, they treated us. But they were like-- they used like those whips-- work, work, work, work. We were working whatever we could do, you know? What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, just digging ditches-- in the field, digging ditches.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: For one the-- just to keep everybody-- just one-- that was one day, Tuesday, every Tuesday. The kids that were 12 years old, they had to go [INAUDIBLE]. But before, like the, the kids were together. They used to train like soldier, like, you know, kids, but then only to work in the fields that day-- digging ditches. And they used to beat you-- just, rush, rush. Not, you know, not just everybody. They used to beat you- rush, rush, rush, rush. Never was enough fast enough to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you say to your, your mother or your dad when you went home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. What can you say? You can't say nothing to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They know. We tell them, sure. But they couldn't do nothing. They-- you-- they couldn't do nothing. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: So every Tuesday you went to this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every Tuesday and Saturday, because they knew Sab-- Saturday was the Jewish Sabbath. They took every Saturday, took the Jewish men and the kids special to dig ditches, just to, just to, just to disrupt the Sabbath. Every Saturday, just to dig ditches.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- your grandfather was still living with you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather was still alive, but he died just before the-- about a couple years after the Hungarians came. Because they used to-- see, whoever was not born there, the Hungarians-- my father-- grandfather was born of Polish descent. His grandfather is foreign. Whoever was not real Hungarian, Czech, or were they born there, they tried to chase them, send them out. They did. Took a lot of people, they just, they took them out. Matter of fact, they used to take them up, some parts of the [? Dniester, ?] they used to call it, a river someplace in Hungary. And they threw them in the river, I heard. They the told the-- some people escaped. And they came back, that they took a lot of pe-- they killed a lot of people. And they threw them in the [? Dniester. ?] We couldn't believe that somebody escaped from there and told us about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your grandfather go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my grandfather kept-- stayed there for awhile. And my uncle, he used to send the, the mayor of the town-- whatever you call it-- you know, the, the head of the town, he used to bribe him. Every week, he used to give him a basket of fruit or cakes that he should let my grandfather stay. Because otherwise, every week he had a deportation letter to leave. So this--

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was for Jews who were not--

speaker: interviewee
text: Who were not--

speaker: interviewer
text: Native Czechoslovakians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Na-- native Czechoslovakian or Hungarian. At that time it was Hungary, Hungary, really. So my grandf-- my uncle, he used to give-- every week, he used to send. Matter of fact, I used to take it over to the main, to the like city hall, it was, to the head of the-- you know, the city hall. I used to give him a basket of fruit or food or anything like this. He should leave my grandfather alone. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you? Was it-- you were a young boy to go into the city hall to, to hope that your grandfather wouldn't be taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was hoping they shouldn't bother him. You know, but finally, finally he died. A few years after, he died. So was no more left for him. But there was-- they-- you know, we used to live together with them. And when they came, and all of a sudden, this everything changed. They used to-- Jew [INAUDIBLE], Jew, especially on Christmas Eve. Now before that, we didn't know nothing about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know there was Christmas, you know? And af-- but the Hungarians already, they, they came out of the church Christmas Eve. Nobody went out. No Jewish people went out from the house. Because they used to come out. They used to-- right away, used to break windows, the Jew, Jew, Jew this and-- every Christmas. They used to blame everything on the Jews. And before that, we used to get along with them fine. We didn't have any problem with them. And then, even then already, the kids that went to school with us, even they started already with Jew this and Jew this, you know? But before, we never any problems with them. Did you have to identify yourself as a Jewish person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, then they, they were, they had-- about couple years after, we had to wear the yellow star. That was already a couple years before they took us away. We had to wear a yellow star. Otherwise-- the, the-- we had to. You know, we had to wear the yellow star to identify that we're Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you kids say amongst yourselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't say too much, because it was just going on with the life. And we were young. What could you say?

speaker: interviewer
text: When your mother was sewing things on your, on your clothes, did she say anything at that point when she was sewing the stars on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she sewed it on. We just-- we had to put it on. And that's all. But just-- no, but life was just like, you know, if you're discriminated against, you know, you just-- and you don't think why. You can't imagine why. Because before, we used to get along fine with everybody. We never had any problem with anybody. Matter of fact, they used to come and to bring-- the, the fruits and vegetables used to come. They knew us already. They left. And we paid them. They went their own way. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was when you had the grocery store.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And there were no problems. They used to come in. They sat in the store. They used to come and eat. We had never any problems till, till they started the agitation, agitating, Jew and Jew this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you-- did you get news about the war? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to get news from the war, the people that used to escape from there. But you know, they deported those Pole-- the Jews that wasn't born there, was born-- some of them used to escape and came back. But we couldn't believe it. We couldn't believe it. They came back. One of them, I remember, came back. She said, you know, they took the people. And they threw them off from the bridge. They threw them right into the water, with the deep, the deep river there. And it was the [INAUDIBLE], the [? Dniester ?], they used to call it. I don't know what part of the country it was. But they throw them in, in the river there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear any other news of any other war, like when they were bombing in London, or, or when the Japanese bombed Pearl Harbor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we knew about the bombing for London. But the war was going on, yeah. We know that. But it was not-- you know, it was-- radio, we didn't have. Because they took away from every Jew, they took away the radio. We couldn't have any radio. As soon as they came in, after about a year, they confiscated every radio from the Jewish people. You had to turn--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know you were Jewish? How did they track that, that you were a Jewish family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll tell you. I'll tell you what happened. Even-- it, it got so bad that the pe-- iif they wouldn't even know they would give you away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would give you away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Your neighbors, like let me tell you an instance. Like, they took us away. That's already-- after, when the Germans were in already. We [INAUDIBLE]--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in 1944, when they took us to the concentration camp, before the conc-- we, they came and got us together. The pol-- the gendarmerie came and one German. And we clean up-- we went into the attic. And I brought something down, because we had to leave, pack up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the teacher from the-- yeah, before that, they took away our house. They made a kindergarten out of it, the house. We had to move downstairs. So upstairs, they rented the-- they took the house for a kindergarten, the state.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I went when the day when they took us away, the teacher was there. And I took something from the attic to pack. She says to the gendarmerie, to the police there, she says, make sure nothing is left in the attic, nobody was hiding in the attic. Even the police were laughing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were the police laughing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she said-- she, she's supposed to be a teacher teaching children. She was not Jewish. She said, make sure the attic check. They shouldn't hide over there. Somebody shouldn't hide. So that's why you couldn't hide. Because they would, they would find out anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were very unfriendly to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: After [INAUDIBLE], because they were agitate, agitating them. They were coming out every time, Jew this and Jew this. You know, it was just-- before, we were friendly. Before-- and all of a sudden, they, they started to agitate them, you know? So they--

speaker: interviewer
text: So until-- Did you have enough to eat prior to 1944? Was-- did you, did you have enough? Your family was together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because, I, I was lucky. Me, [? I was lucky. ?] I used to work as I told you, worked in a bakery. So I used to bring home a loaf of bread. So and I-- my family had bread. You know, and then we have potatoes. So we used to cook potatoes and beans and bread. We had enough bread. Because I brought it home, because I work in a bakery.

speaker: interviewer
text: What then happened in 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944, that's when, in Passover, that when the Germans came in-- 1944, exactly the first day of Passover. On the day before Passover, that night, the Germans marched in. And they disrupted everything. With Passover, you know, we're not allowed to have bread in the house or things. They came in. And all the houses, they was disrupting. They put their, you know, their non-kosher food on the stove, and bread. And they disrupted the whole Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it during a seder? Were you having a seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the seder. We couldn't have a seder. Because they were disrupting everything. So we just celebrated and no food. We didn't eat no food, anything. And, and then they-- they [? were there a ?] week, the Germans. Just the last day of Passover, I went in to bake bread. Because I was at the bakery. The main baker could not come in. He was-- he lived in the city. He couldn't come in across the bridge, because they locked up all the routes from all over. He couldn't come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this is a Jewish bakery, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah, it was Jewish bakery. And he came-- he couldn't come in. So I did whatever I could. I-- the night-- we used to bake at night mostly, to deliver [INAUDIBLE]. I, I got a hold of the little kid, a Gypsy, he was a Gypsy. And I baked two, two, two, two ovens full of bread for the town, you know, to be enough. Then we distributed it the, the next day after Passover. And at noon, when I came back at noon, that's when the, the, the Germans came and took us away. They took us to the main school.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the main school in--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the main schoolyard.

speaker: interviewer
text: In in, in Munkc, or in the other--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munkc and [INAUDIBLE]. You got the main school in [INAUDIBLE]. And then the afternoon, they marched us over to Munkc. That was the city. They put us in the brick factory. That's where they baked the br-- you know, make the brick from clay. And they bake it in that-- in those, like, ovens in there. And the front of the ovens, there was like [INAUDIBLE]. You know, like they used to build, like, just like arches. And that's where they used to go in to bake the, the bricks. That's where they put us in there to-- in the front. They put us there. We slept on the ground there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole town, the whole tow-- a lot of people were there, a lot. The whole town and part of the city-- it was a big town, and from the city, too, everybody. And as soon as we got there the first day, they took mostly the Jewish people, the, the religious people that day. The next day-- you know, the religious people, they wear beards. And they have payots. You know what payots are-- sideburns, like. And they took pictures. The Germans took pictures. And they took shears, you know, not scissors or-- they cut the beards like. And they got a hold of the, the, the sideburn, just tear it out, [INAUDIBLE] together. And they took pictures with it, movie pictures. And they were laughing. They were having fun. Let's look at this. Look at this. They could send home to their mommy, you know, to show what we could do to Jews. And then they used to take every single day-- they, first they took some kids from the Jewish kids. They take axes, took axes like-- and they beat the hell out of them. He says, now you're going to follow what I tell you. You take those people. You gonna chase them. Otherwise, we're going to beat you. They took the Jewish people, mostly religious. They give them two bricks in their hand. And they tell them, you have to run with the bricks. You have to run. And they make pictures like that, taking them with the bricks, and running as fast-- they used to be chasing them, fast chasing and make-- and run them every single day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once, once I did. But the rest-- but mostly, they took the real religious people, the, the rabbis [INAUDIBLE], Those people most of the time, they, they took them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your family that whole

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I stayed with my mother. My father wasn't there, because they took him in the, in the labor camp before. And my brother wasn't there. Just the day they took us away, he was drafted into the Hungarian labor camp also, because he was just of age when they drafted people. He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know where your father went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know where he went. But I know that the men, they, they-- the men, they took into-- the men, they took into the labor camp before. Because they were working, like in you know, like a labor camp, like, like concentration. But it was not. It was Hungarian camp, labor camp. So that, That was the last day that we were together. And then we were there in that brick factory for about a week or so, two weeks. I don't remember exactly. Then the trains came in. And they, and they put us on the train.
